_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 7-5 CPAP Administration (Revised 11/17/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
level_display: Procedure (Specialist base; EMT scope at MCAs with cpap_bls expansion)
protocol_reference: Michigan Procedures Protocol Section 7-5: CPAP Administration — MDHHS Approved 11/17/2025
purpose: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) for patients who have not responded to initial treatment, meet one or more protocol indications, and have no contraindications. Specialist-level skill in base Michigan protocol; EMT-authorized at MCAs with the cpap_bls expansion. The CPAP machine must be capable of adjusting pressure in cmH2O minimally up to 10 cmH2O.

sections.title: Indications
points: Severe respiratory distress that has not responded to initial treatment
Congestive heart failure / pulmonary edema
Pulmonary edema / near-drowning
Hypoxia: SpO2 < 92% after application of supplemental oxygen
Acute exacerbation of asthma/COPD
Patient must be awake and able to follow instructions to maintain mask seal

sections.title: Contraindications
points: Cardiac or respiratory arrest
Uncorrected hypotension: adult > 10 years SBP < 90 mmHg; pediatric < 10 years SBP < 70 + (2 × age) mmHg
Small adult CPAP mask does not properly fit the patient and/or pediatric size CPAP mask is not available
Inability to maintain patent airway, including poor secretion clearance and/or unresponsiveness
Major trauma, known or suspected pneumothorax, penetrating or blunt chest trauma, or blast injury
Active vomiting or gastrointestinal bleeding with ongoing emesis
Significant facial trauma or burns affecting mask placement

sections.title: Administration
points: Specialist and Paramedic (base Michigan); EMT authorized at MCAs with cpap_bls expansion
Equipment: CPAP device must be adjustable minimally up to at least 10 cmH2O
Starting pressure: 5 cmH2O
Titrate pressure as necessary and as tolerated based on patient response, SpO2, and work of breathing
Monitor vital signs every 10 minutes; every 5 minutes on short transport
The CPAP mask may be briefly removed to allow administration of oral or sublingual medications
Contact Medical Control and consider sedation to reduce anxiety per Patient Procedural Sedation-Procedure Protocol
Do NOT remove CPAP upon arrival at hospital until hospital respiratory therapy is ready to continue treatment
CPAP may be used on patients with a valid DNR who are NOT in cardiac arrest

sections.title: Discontinuation Criteria
points: Patient condition deteriorates despite CPAP — reassess and consider advanced airway
Patient cannot tolerate CPAP device
Vomiting risk becomes present or patient begins vomiting
Signs of pneumothorax develop
Cardiac or respiratory arrest occurs — remove mask, begin CPR and airway management per appropriate protocol

deterioration_flags: SpO2 < 92% worsening despite CPAP — reassess mask seal, pressure setting; consider advanced airway
SBP drops below 90 (adult) during CPAP — discontinue CPAP; treat hypotension per appropriate protocol
Patient becomes unconscious or unable to maintain airway — remove CPAP; manage airway per Airway Management procedure
Signs of tension pneumothorax develop — remove CPAP immediately; needle decompression per Pleural Decompression procedure (Paramedic)
scope_notes: CPAP administration: Specialist and Paramedic (base Michigan); EMT scope at MCAs with cpap_bls expansion
CPAP machine must adjust cmH2O minimally up to at least 10 cmH2O
Starting pressure: 5 cmH2O; titrate as necessary and tolerated
Do not remove CPAP on hospital arrival until hospital respiratory therapy ready to continue
CPAP may be used on DNR patients who are NOT in arrest
